title: 2 Kings 21
subtitle: Manasseh — The Point of No Return

sections.header: Verses 1–18 — 55 Years of Apostasy; Prophets Declare Irreversible Judgment

heb.paragraph: Manasseh “passed his son through the fire” (v.6), practised sorcery and divination, and consulted mediums and spiritists. The catalogue of abominations is comprehensive — every category of prohibited practice in Deuteronomy 18:10–12 is represented.

panels.ctx: Manasseh reigns 55 years — the longest reign of any king — and undoes everything Hezekiah built. He rebuilds the high places, erects Baal altars and an Asherah pole in the temple, worships the starry hosts, sacrifices his son, practises sorcery, and fills Jerusalem with innocent blood “from end to end.” The prophets declare: “I am going to bring such disaster on Jerusalem and Judah that the ears of everyone who hears of it will tingle.” God will “wipe Jerusalem as one wipes a dish.” Manasseh’s 55 years determine Judah’s fate: even Josiah’s reform cannot undo this damage (23:26–27).

cross.note: “Still the LORD did not turn away from the heat of his fierce anger... because of all that Manasseh had done.” Even Josiah’s reform cannot reverse Manasseh’s damage.
“I will make them abhorrent to all the kingdoms of the earth because of what Manasseh son of Hezekiah king of Judah did in Jerusalem.” Jeremiah cites Manasseh by name as the cause.
Chronicles adds that Manasseh repented in Assyrian captivity. The Deuteronomistic historian omits this — his narrative requires Manasseh to be the irredeemable cause of exile.

notes.note: Manasseh’s sins demonstrate that evil has no bottom. Those who abandon God descend progressively into darker practices. The trajectory of apostasy is always downward, and there is always something worse below.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The “measuring line of Samaria and the plumb line of Ahab” uses construction metaphors for destruction: God will demolish Jerusalem with the same precision he used to judge Samaria.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Manasseh is mentioned in Assyrian records as a vassal of both Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal. The Prism of Esarhaddon lists “Manasi king of Judah” (Ma-na-si-i shar Ia-u-di) among tribute-paying kings. His long reign reflects willing submission to Assyrian suzerainty.

The “starry hosts” (tseba hashsahmayim) refers to astral worship imported from Mesopotamia. Assyrian suzerainty brought Assyrian religion; Manasseh’s astral worship was likely a political-religious concession to his Assyrian overlords.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The “measuring line” and “plumb line” are instruments of both construction and destruction. God built Jerusalem with these tools; now he will demolish it with the same. The Creator becomes the Destroyer when creation rejects its Creator.

sections.header: Verses 19–26 — Amon: Assassinated After Two Years

panels.ctx: Amon succeeds Manasseh and continues his practices for two years before his own officials assassinate him. The “people of the land” (landowners, traditional power base) kill the conspirators and enthrone eight-year-old Josiah. The transition is rapid and violent — two verses for a king who made no difference.

cross.note: Joash was also assassinated by his officials. The pattern recurs: unfaithful kings die by internal conspiracy.

notes.note: The enthronement of a child-king teaches that God’s purposes do not depend on human maturity or readiness. He places eight-year-old Josiah on the throne because age is no barrier to God’s calling.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: ʿam hāʾārets (“people of the land”) — a socio-political term for the landed nobility or free citizenry who wielded influence in state affairs, distinct from both the court aristocracy and the urban populace.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The “people of the land” (ʿam haʾārets) represent the landed gentry of Judah — a politically conservative class that appears at critical moments in the monarchy’s history to ensure dynastic continuity.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The “people of the land” function as guardians of the Davidic succession. Their intervention at moments of crisis (here and in 11:14, 18–20) reveals that the dynasty survives not by its own strength but through the loyalty of its traditional base.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI will not again make the feet of the Israelites wander from the land I gave their ancestors, if only they will be careful to do everything I commanded them and will keep the whole Law that my servant Moses gave them.\u201dESVAnd I will not cause the feet of Israel to wander anymore out of the land that I gave to their fathers, if only they will be careful to do according to all that I have commanded them, and according to all the Law that my servant Moses commanded them.\u201dNIVHe did evil in the eyes of the Lord, following the detestable practices of the nations the Lord had driven out before the Israelites.ESVAnd he did what was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to the despicable practices of the nations whom the LORD drove out before the people of Israel.
